sections.title: What are tenses? (1)
sections.content: In English, **tense** tells us **when** something happens. We combine **three times** (past, present, future) with **four aspects** (simple, continuous, perfect, perfect continuous) to get **12** tense forms.

sections.title: What are tenses? (2)
sections.content: English uses **auxiliary verbs** (*do*, *have*, *be*, *will*) to build these forms — it doesn't change verb endings for each person (except 3rd person present: *he works*).

sections.title: Simple aspect (1)
sections.content: **Simple aspect** — Fact, habit, or single completed event — no focus on duration or completion. **Present simple:** *I work every day. She likes coffee.* **Past simple:** *I worked yesterday. She left at 5.*

sections.title: Simple aspect (2)
sections.content: **Future simple:** *I will help you. She is going to study medicine.* **Structure:** base verb (present), *-ed* or irregular (past), *will* + base (future).

sections.title: Continuous aspect (1)
sections.content: **Continuous aspect** — Action in progress at a given time. **Structure:** *be* + *-ing*. **Present continuous:** *I am working right now.* **Past continuous:** *I was working when she called.*

sections.title: Continuous aspect (2)
sections.content: **Future continuous:** *I will be working at 5 tomorrow.* Also used for temporary situations and background actions.

sections.title: Perfect aspect (1)
sections.content: **Perfect aspect** — Completed action with result or relevance at a later time. **Structure:** *have/has/had* + past participle. **Present perfect:** *I have finished. She has been to Japan.* **Past perfect:** *I had finished when she arrived.*

sections.title: Perfect aspect (2)
sections.content: **Future perfect:** *I will have finished by 5.* Often used with *for*, *since*, *by*, *already*, *yet*, *ever*, *never*.

sections.title: Perfect continuous aspect (1)
sections.content: **Perfect continuous aspect** — Action lasting over a period, often with visible result. **Structure:** *have/has/had been* + *-ing*.

sections.title: Perfect continuous aspect (2)
sections.content: **Present perfect continuous:** *I have been waiting for an hour.* **Past perfect continuous:** *I had been waiting for an hour when she came.*

sections.title: Perfect continuous aspect (3)
sections.content: **Future perfect continuous:** *By next month I will have been working here for ten years.* Focus on **duration**.

sections.title: How to choose the right tense (1)
sections.content: Ask two questions: **When?** (past, present, future) → this gives you the time. **How do I view the action?** → this gives you the aspect.

sections.title: How to choose the right tense (2)
sections.content: Fact or habit = **simple**. In progress right now = **continuous**. Completed with a result = **perfect**. Lasting over a period with a result = **perfect continuous**.

sections.title: Typical errors (1)
sections.content: **Wrong:** *I am work every day.* **Right:** **I work** every day (simple) or **I am working** now (continuous). **Wrong:** *She have finished.* **Right:** **She has** finished. **Wrong:** *I did went* yesterday. **Right:** **I went** yesterday.

sections.title: Typical errors (2)
sections.content: **Wrong:** *I am waiting* for an hour. **Right:** **I have been waiting** for an hour (duration → perfect continuous).

sections.title: Reference
sections.content: For detailed rules on each tense, see the individual topics under Choose Grammar Topic (Past Simple, Present Perfect, Past Perfect, etc.).

questions.question: How do we get the 12 tense forms in English?
questions.explanation: We combine **3 times** (present, past, future) with **4 aspects** (simple, continuous, perfect, perfect continuous) to get 12 forms.

questions.question: In English, how do we usually form questions and negatives in most tenses?

options.a: By changing the verb ending for each person
options.b: By using auxiliary verbs (do, does, did, have, will, be, etc.)
options.c: By adding a suffix to the main verb
options.d: By changing the word order only

questions.explanation: English uses **auxiliary verbs** (do, does, did, have, has, had, will, am/is/are, was/were) to form questions, negatives, and many positive forms.

questions.question: What is **aspect** in English grammar?

options.a: The person (I, you, he/she) of the subject
options.b: The way we look at the action in time (e.g. in progress, completed)
options.c: The time of day when something happens
options.d: Whether the sentence is positive or negative

questions.explanation: **Aspect** is how we view the action in time: e.g. a single event, in progress, completed (with a result), or lasting over a period.

questions.question: Which aspect do we use when the action is **in progress** at a given time?
questions.explanation: **Continuous (progressive)** – the action is in progress. We use **be + -ing** (e.g. I am working, she was leaving).

questions.question: 'I have been waiting for an hour.' – Which tense form is this?
questions.explanation: **Present perfect continuous**: have/has been + -ing. We focus on how long something has been going on (duration).

questions.question: 'She had left before I arrived.' – Which tense form is this?
questions.explanation: **Past perfect simple**: had + past participle. Something completed **before** another time or action in the past.

questions.question: Which aspect focuses on **completion** and often on the **result** or relevance at a later time?
questions.explanation: **Perfect** – we focus on completion and result/relevance. We use have/has/had + past participle (e.g. I have finished, she had left).

questions.question: How does English typically express time and aspect?

options.a: English uses one word per tense.
options.b: English often uses auxiliaries + main verb (e.g. I have been waiting); time and aspect are shown by structure.

questions.explanation: **English** uses auxiliaries and often two or more words (e.g. I have been waiting). Time and aspect are shown by the verb structure, not by a single conjugated form.
